last_excerpt: ever misread a barometer on purpose, wanting a storm or an end to one? --- Non-answers: — An old blue jug sits by the sink, full of tulips and water going cloudy. — No two curtains move the same way in an open window’s breeze. — The cat sleeps with one ear open, one folded flat. — The door swells and sticks after rain. — The thermometer by the post office is always wrong at dawn. — Silver spoons ring out against each other in the drawer when the truck rumbles past. — Someone wakes in a different mood and attributes it to the moon. — The stairs creak harder when you take them in the dark. — Leftover soup lets off a spiral of steam before settling. — The bread dough turns sticky before a thunderstorm. — There is a faint greenish light under the clouds before it rains.
last_concepts: the way a pantograph copies a shape at a different scale
the way a tuning fork produces a pure tone
the way a draft in a chimney pulls air upward by being warmer than outside
how a barometer measures the weight of the air
